utterances: He says he saw 200 armored vehicles, he estimates, that were deployed to try and put down this protest separately.
He says there were 400 to 500 paramilitary police and they were trying to persuade people to leave.
Those who didn't were detained.
And the eyewitness that we spoke to said that many of the people who were detained were later released, but that they had been very obviously beaten whilst in police custody.
There's been more debate on the one-child policy in China recently, what's the current status of that law?
The government has said that it will continue to uphold the one-child policy.
But there is, as you say, quite open debate about the extent to which it really is being upheld.
In a lot of cases in the cities, people who are rich or famous, the sort of superstars, are paying the fine in order to have more than one child.
And so, ordinary people are really angered by this.
They see this as a symbol of the inequality of life in China, the wealth gap that, you know, rich people really can buy their way out of trouble and they're above the law.
But at the same time, there are quite a lot of people who do still support the one-child policy.
They agree with the government's line that China's population is too large.
And they do support the view that in order to guarantee China's economic development and its future prosperity, there should be caps on its population.
So the view is still quite mixed.
NPR's Louisa Lim in Shanghai.
Thanks so much for joining us.
Thank you.
This is TALK OF THE NATION.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
An outsider upset in Florida; an out-of-the-blue, too-close-to-call in Alaska; and in Arizona, Quayle spells victory.

Every Wednesday, NPR political editor Ken Rudin joins us to review the week in politics.
Two wealthy outsiders shook up primaries in Florida this year.

Incumbent Senators John McCain and Pat Leahy sailed to re-nomination, but the shocker of the day remains undecided.

These confirmation hearings on Ms. Harriet Miers are scheduled to begin on Monday, November 7th.
It has not been easy coming to a date because it was only yesterday that we received the response to the questionnaire, and Senator Leahy and I took a look at it and agreed that it was insufficient.
A number of the questions really weren't answered at all.
Again, not to play gotcha, but we have a responsibility to 98 other senators who then have a responsibility to every person in this country.

If the Senate decides to discriminate against our state to take money only from our state, I'll resign from this body if one senator can decide he'll take all the money from one state to solve a problem of another.

So you can get perspective on this, $230 million for 50 people where there's a ferry service already running every 15 to 30 minutes--it takes seven minutes to cross--is enough money to buy each one of them a Learjet.

Donald Trump met with his foreign policy team yesterday in Washington.
One participant says Trump mostly listened but also asked some pointed questions.
Now, the Republican front-runner has said a lot about foreign policy.
And foreign policy specialists have a lot they want to say about that.
NPR's Scott Horsley reports.
When Donald Trump looks at America's standing in the world, he doesn't see a superpower.
Trump told CNN this week, he sees a chump.
(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING)
You look at what the world is doing to us at every level, whether it's militarily or in trade or in so many other levels.
The world is taking advantage of the United States.
And it's driving us into, literally, being a third-world nation.
Trump's response is a curious combination of fight and flight.
On the one hand, he says he wants to bomb the stuffing out of ISIS, or words to that effect.
On the other, he's talked about withdrawing U.S.
military protection from Japan, South Korea and Saudi Arabia unless those countries agree to shoulder a much larger share of the cost.
(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING)
The United States cannot afford to be the policeman of the world anymore, folks.
We have to rebuild our own country.
Trump has embraced a name for his policy, America First, a label that was also used in the 1940s by isolationists bent on keeping this country out of World War II.
His approach alarms many mainstream foreign policy experts, including, Daniel Drezner of the Fletcher School of Diplomacy.
My first response is to go to the liquor cabinet immediately and try to feel better that way.
Drezner is on of more than a hundred Republican foreign policy practitioners who signed an open letter opposing Trump.
In some respects, though, Trump is just saying loudly what others mutter under their breath.
Take for example Trump's complaint in that CNN town hall about NATO.
(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING)
You have countries in NATO that are getting a free ride, and it's unfair.
Perhaps most surprisingly is the extent to which he overlaps with some of President Obama's positions.
Peter Feaver worked in the National Security Council for both George W.
Bush and Bill Clinton.

He notes President Obama has also complained, quietly, about NATO partners who don't spend enough on their own defense, as well as countries in the Middle East that are all too willing to have the U.S.
fight their battles for them.
Where there's a sharp difference, of course, is that President Obama wanted to do less in the Middle East in order to more in Asia.
And Trump is talking about doing less in the Middle East and less in Asia.
Trump's approach to foreign policy is transactional, with a constant eye on what the U.S.
stands to gain.
Speaking to a pro-Israel group, he likened talks with the Palestinians to the kind of business negotiation he's famous for.
(SOUNDBITE OF ARCHIVED RECORDING)
You see, I know about deal-making.
That's what I do.
I wrote ""The Art Of The Deal.
Drezner argues Trump is shortsighted, though, in approaching international deal-making as a zero-sum game, where if China's getting ahead, for example, the U.S.
must be falling behind.
He fails to realize that there's a lot of win-win agreements in the world and that by threatening to drive harder bargains, he might manage to eke out a slightly larger share of the pie.
But he also threatens to blow up that pie in the process.
